name: Bhairav
aroha: Sa Re̱ Ga Ma Pa Dha̱ Ni Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni Dha̱ Pa Ma Ga Re̱ Sa
pakad: Ga Ma Dha̱ Dha̱ Pa, Ga Ma Re̱ Re̱ Sa
mood: Solemn, meditative, profound — a sunrise raga of philosophical depth, often the opening piece of a concert.
notes: The defining raga of its thaat, with komal Re and komal Dha sung in a slow oscillation (andolan) that gives it gravity. One of the few ragas said to suit any season. Its rasa is described as shaant aur gambhir — serene and grave.

name: Ahir Bhairav
aroha: Sa Re̱ Ga Ma Pa Dha Ni̱ Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni̱ Dha Pa Ma Ga Re̱ Sa
pakad: Sa, Re̱ Ga Ma, Ga Ma Re̱, Ni̱̣ Dhạ, Ni̱̣ Re̱ Sa
mood: Tender and devotional, with a gentle gravity — softer and more lyrical than Bhairav itself.
notes: A blend of Bhairav and the old raga Ahiri: it keeps Bhairav's komal Re (with a light oscillation) but uses shuddha Dha and komal Ni, lending a warmth that has made it a favourite for bhajans and morning film songs.

name: Bhairavi
samay: Morning, but sung to close any concert at any hour
aroha: Sa Re̱ Ga̱ Ma Pa Dha̱ Ni̱ Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni̱ Dha̱ Pa Ma Ga̱ Re̱ Sa
pakad: Ma Ga̱ Re̱ Sa, Ga̱ Ma Dha̱ Pa
mood: Tender, plaintive, devotional — the raga of resolution and farewell.
notes: All four komal (flat) swaras give Bhairavi its soft, pleading colour. By convention it is performed last, releasing the audience gently; in light-classical practice many ghazals and bhajans borrow its phrases.

name: Patdeep
aroha: Ni̱ Sa Ga Ma Pa Ni Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni Dha Pa Ma Ga Re Sa
pakad: Ma Ga, Ma Pa Ga, Re Sa
mood: Warm and stirring — a bright afternoon raga with an undercurrent of pathos.
notes: Resembles Bhimpalasi but uses shuddha (natural) Nishad in ascent against komal Gandhar's softness, giving it a distinct, slightly brighter colour. A pentatonic ascent broadening to a full descent.

name: Bhimpalasi
aroha: Ni̱ Sa Ga̱ Ma Pa Ni̱ Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni̱ Dha Pa Ma Ga̱ Re Sa
pakad: Ni̱ Sa Ma, Ma Ga̱ Re Sa
mood: Tender and yearning — a wistful, lovelorn afternoon raga that suits the ache of separation.
notes: An audava-sampurna raga: five notes ascending (skipping Re and Dha), all seven descending. Both Gandhar and Nishad are komal. Its pull toward longing makes it a natural fit for many ghazals.

name: Yaman
aroha: Ni Re Ga Má Pa Dha Ni Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni Dha Pa Má Ga Re Sa
pakad: Ni Re Ga, Re Ga, Má Pa, Ga Re Sa
mood: Serene, devotional, expansive — often the first raga taught, and the threshold into an evening of music.
notes: Uses the sharp Ma (Ma tivra) of the Kalyan thaat. The full octave is available in both ascent and descent, which gives Yaman its open, unhurried character.

name: Bhupali
aroha: Sa Re Ga Pa Dha Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Dha Pa Ga Re Sa
pakad: Ga Re Sa Dhạ Sa Re Ga, Pa Ga Dha Pa Ga Re Sa
mood: Peaceful, devotional, serene — an open pentatonic raga, often the first scale a student learns to sing.
notes: A five-note (audava) raga of the Kalyan family, dropping Ma and Ni entirely so only natural notes remain. Also called Bhoop. Its simplicity and calm make it a beginner's raga and a perennial favourite for evening singing.

name: Kafi
samay: Late evening; associated with spring and Holi
aroha: Sa Re Ga̱ Ma Pa Dha Ni̱ Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni̱ Dha Pa Ma Ga̱ Re Sa
pakad: Sa Sa Re Re Ga̱ Ga̱ Ma, Pa Dha Ni̱ Sȧ
mood: Light, romantic, folk-tinged — the raga of thumri, dadra, and the playful songs of spring.
notes: Komal Ga and komal Ni over an otherwise natural scale. A semi-classical favourite: relaxed and emotive rather than austere, which is why so much light-classical and ghazal singing lives here.

name: Khamaj
samay: Late evening, second prahar of night
aroha: Sa Ga Ma Pa Dha Ni Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni̱ Dha Pa Ma Ga Re Sa
pakad: Ni̱̣ Dhạ Ma Pa Dha Ma Ga
mood: Romantic, light, expressive — the raga of countless ghazals and thumris.
notes: Shadava in ascent (Re is dropped going up) and uses shuddha Ni rising but komal Ni descending — an asymmetry that gives Khamaj its characteristic flavour. So many ghazals live here that it is almost the signature raga of the form.

name: Desh
aroha: Ni̱̣ Sa Re Ma Pa Ni Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni̱ Dha Pa Dha Ma Ga Re Sa
pakad: Re Ma Pa Ni Sȧ Rė Ni̱ Dha Pa, Ma Ga Re
mood: Flowing and romantic, with a patriotic association — the melody of Vande Mataram.
notes: An audava-sampurna raga: a stepwise pentatonic ascent broadening to all seven notes in descent. Shuddha Ni rising, komal Ni falling. Re is a strong resting note, and the meend (glide) from Ma down to Re is its signature gesture.

name: Shivaranjani
aroha: Sa Re Ga̱ Pa Dha Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Dha Pa Ga̱ Re Sa
pakad: Sa Re Ga̱, Pa Ga̱ Re Sa, Dhạ Sa Re Ga̱
mood: Plaintive and tender, edged with pathos — a wistful night raga that carries longing and gentle melancholy.
notes: A five-note (audava) raga with no Ma or Ni. It is essentially Bhupali with komal (flat) Gandhar in place of the natural one — that single change turns Bhupali's serenity into Shivaranjani's ache, which is why film composers have reached for it again and again in songs of yearning and sorrow.

name: Darbari Kanada
aroha: Sa Re Ga̱ Ma Pa Dha̱ Ni̱ Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Dha̱ Ni̱ Pa Ma Pa Ga̱ Ma Re Sa
pakad: Re Sa, Dha̱ Ni̱ Sa, Ma Pa, Ga̱ Ma Re Sa
mood: Grave, majestic, contemplative — attributed to Tansen, said to have been shaped for Akbar's court.
notes: Famous for the heavy, oscillating andolan on komal Ga. A deep, serious raga that unfolds slowly in the still hours after midnight.

name: Malkauns
aroha: Sa Ga̱ Ma Dha̱ Ni̱ Sȧ
avaroha: Sȧ Ni̱ Dha̱ Ma Ga̱ Ma Ga̱ Sa
pakad: Ma Ga̱ Ma Dha̱ Ni̱ Dha̱ Ma Ga̱ Sa
mood: Grave, meditative, hypnotic — one of the oldest ragas, sung in the small hours after midnight.
notes: A pentatonic (audava) raga with no Re or Pa, all flat notes save Sa. Developed slowly in the lower octave at a deliberate tempo, it is said to have a soothing, intoxicating effect. The Carnatic equivalent is Hindolam.
